audit.area: Backend stack for owner self-service editing on the static Next.js 16 De Kamp directory (ondernemersvandekamp.nl)
audit.recommendation: Adopt Payload CMS v3, installed inside the existing Next.js 16 app, with Postgres (Neon EU, free/$5 tier) and S3-compatible EU object storage (or Vercel Blob) for images. Why: it is the only option that satisfies ALL hard requirements natively in one codebase. (1) Per-owner "edit only your own business" is a first-class Payload feature: access-control functions return a query constraint (e.g. `{ owner: { equals: user.id } }`) so an owner literally cannot read/update any row but their own — enforced server-side, not just UI. (2) Moderation/approval is built in via Payload's Drafts & Versions: owner saves create a draft version; nothing goes live until the admin clicks Publish. Photo uploads land in an Uploads collection you can also gate on approval. (3) It runs INSIDE the Next app (Payload v3 is a Next plugin — same deploy, no separate service, no CORS), and its content is read in your Server Components via the Local API. Combined with Next 16 on-demand `revalidateTag`/`revalidatePath` fired from a Payload afterChange hook, an approved edit re-renders just that one /ondernemers/[id] page — so the site stays SSG/ISR-fast with intact JSON-LD and Core Web Vitals. (4) The Business type is code-first TypeScript, so your existing ~40-field model maps almost 1:1 onto a Payload collection config and keeps generated types flowing into the frontend. (5) MIT-licensed, self-hosted, no per-seat fees — critical because ~96 owner logins on a per-seat SaaS (Sanity/Contentful) would be the dominant cost. (6) EU/GDPR: you choose the region (Neon EU + EU storage + EU app region), full data sovereignty, no Enterprise upsell needed. Migration is a one-time script that reads businesses.ts and seeds the collection; after that businesses.ts is retired as the source of truth.

options.name: (a) Payload CMS v3 (runs inside Next) + Neon Postgres EU + S3/Blob
options.pros: Single codebase/deploy (Next plugin) so no extra service, CORS, or ops; native per-row access control = true 'edit only your own business'; built-in Drafts/Versions = exact admin approve-before-live workflow; full admin UI AND owner-facing edit UI generated for free; code-first TS collection maps onto existing 40-field Business type; pairs with Next 16 revalidateTag hook to keep SSG/ISR + JSON-LD + CWV; MIT, no per-seat fees (96 owners free); you pick EU region for full GDPR sovereignty; image upload + access-gated uploads collection out of the box.
options.cons: Now a stateful app (needs always-on DB + a non-static deploy target for the admin/API; pure static export no longer covers the whole app); you own upgrades/migrations; slightly more initial setup than a hosted SaaS; admin UI is general-purpose (some polish needed to make the owner experience friendly).

options.name: (b) Supabase (Postgres + Auth + Storage), EU region
options.pros: RLS gives genuine per-row 'edit only your own business' at the DB layer; Auth (magic-link/invite) and Storage (image upload) included; EU regions on all tiers; predictable $25/mo Pro; mature, well-documented; reads cleanly into Server Components and pairs with revalidateTag to keep ISR.
options.cons: No editing UI for owners and no moderation UI for admin — you must BUILD the entire owner dashboard, image-upload widget, the draft/approval workflow, and the admin moderation screens yourself in Next; that's the bulk of the work and the bulk of ongoing maintenance for a solo operator; free tier pauses after a week idle (must be on Pro).

options.name: (c) Next + Prisma + Neon Postgres + Auth.js + Vercel Blob
options.pros: Maximum control and lowest hard cost (Neon free/$5, Auth.js free, Blob cheap); all EU-selectable; stays in one Next app; type-safe with Prisma.
options.cons: The most DIY of all: you hand-build auth wiring, role/row authorization, the moderation/approval state machine, owner edit forms, upload handling, and admin UI from scratch — highest build effort and highest long-term solo-maintenance burden; you reinvent exactly what Payload gives for free; easy to get authorization subtly wrong (security risk).

options.name: (d) Git-based CMS (TinaCMS / Decap) committing to businesses.ts
options.pros: Keeps businesses.ts as source of truth so almost zero change to current SSG pipeline; content versioned in git; cheapest; no DB to run; edits naturally flow through a build.
options.cons: Per-owner 'edit only my row' is awkward/unsafe — Git CMS grants access at repo/branch/PR granularity, not row level; no clean way to stop one owner editing another's listing or the rest of the file; moderation works only as PR review (heavy for non-technical owners); every owner edit triggers a FULL site rebuild (no single-page ISR); image upload commits binaries into the repo; Decap is only lightly maintained (quiet repo, OAuth/GitHub coupling); poor fit for 96 non-technical owners.

options.name: Sanity / Contentful / Strapi / Directus (other headless CMS)
options.pros: Sanity/Contentful: polished hosted editing; Strapi/Directus: self-hostable, Directus has true field/row-level permissions and EU self-host.
options.cons: Sanity gates EU data residency behind Enterprise (GDPR strike) and is per-seat (~$15/seat) — 96 owner logins is cost-prohibitive; Contentful Team is ~$300/mo — out of scope for a district directory; Strapi/Directus are separate services to run/secure (extra ops + CORS + a second deploy) and Strapi's row-level/owner-scoped permissions are weaker; Directus is the closest of these but still a separate app to maintain vs Payload living inside the existing Next codebase.

decisions.question: Which single stack should the client adopt?
decisions.answer: Payload CMS v3 inside the existing Next.js app, backed by Neon Postgres (EU) and EU/S3-compatible image storage. It is the only option that delivers per-owner row scoping, admin approve-before-live moderation, image upload, and an owner+admin UI natively without building it all by hand.

decisions.question: What is the runner-up?
decisions.answer: Supabase (Postgres + Auth + Storage, EU region). It gives airtight per-row security (RLS) and the building blocks cheaply, but you must build the owner dashboard, upload flow, and the entire moderation/approval workflow yourself — significantly more solo-maintenance than Payload. Choose it only if you specifically want DB-layer RLS and are happy to build the app UI.

decisions.question: Can the site stay SSG/ISR and SEO-strong with a DB/CMS backend?
decisions.answer: Yes. Next.js 16 supports on-demand revalidation (revalidateTag / revalidatePath, Cache Components model). Pages render statically and are cached; a Payload afterChange/publish hook fires revalidateTag for that one listing, so only the affected /ondernemers/[id] (and any list/category pages) re-render. JSON-LD, sitemap, and Core Web Vitals are unaffected.

decisions.question: What happens to src/data/businesses.ts?
decisions.answer: It becomes a one-time seed source. Write a migration script that imports the existing array and inserts each record into the Payload Business collection (the collection config mirrors the current ~40-field type). After migration the DB is the source of truth and the file can be retired (or kept as a versioned backup/export target).

decisions.question: How are the ~96 owners onboarded without per-seat cost?
decisions.answer: Payload is MIT/self-hosted with no per-seat fees, so all 96 owner accounts are free. Admin invites an owner (creates a user linked to their Business row); owner logs in (email/password or magic link) and sees only their listing. This is the decisive cost reason Sanity/Contentful are ruled out — they bill per editor seat.

steps: Spike: install Payload v3 into the existing Next.js 16 app (Payload ships as a Next plugin — admin lives at /admin in the same deploy). Confirm it boots against a local Postgres.

Define the Business collection in Payload TypeScript config, mirroring the current Business interface (~40 fields incl. structured DayHours, perfectFor, imageStatus/verificationStatus etc.). Add an `owner` relationship field and a `status`/draft flag. Enable Drafts & Versions on the collection for the approve-before-live workflow.

Configure access control: read = published only for the public; update/read of a row = admin OR the matching owner (return `{ owner: { equals: user.id } }`). Verify an owner cannot touch another owner's row.

Set up an Uploads collection (or S3 adapter) for images pointing at EU object storage (S3-compatible EU bucket, or Vercel Blob); gate publish of new photos behind admin approval; keep imageFit/imageStatus semantics.
Write and run a one-time migration script: import { businesses } from src/data/businesses.ts and create one Payload doc per business (published state, owner unset until invited).

Replace the direct `import { businesses }` in the ~16 consumers with a data layer that reads from Payload's Local API inside Server Components, wrapped in `use cache` + cacheTag per business; keep generateStaticParams driven by the published set.

Add an afterChange/afterPublish hook that calls revalidateTag(`business:<id>`) (and the relevant list/category/sitemap tags) so an approved edit re-renders only the affected pages — preserving SSG/ISR, JSON-LD, and CWV.

Build the admin moderation view (Payload's Versions UI gives diff + publish; add a simple 'pending changes' filter) and a lightweight, friendly owner edit screen (Payload admin works, optionally skin it).

Deploy: Next app + Payload to an EU region (e.g. Vercel EU / a Hetzner/Fly EU box), Neon Postgres EU, EU image bucket. Confirm GDPR posture (DPA in place with each provider, EU data residency end-to-end).

QA the full loop: admin invites owner -> owner edits text/hours/photo -> change sits as draft -> admin approves -> only that listing re-renders live. Re-verify Lighthouse/CWV and structured data are unchanged.
risks: Architecture shift: the app stops being a purely static export and becomes a stateful Next+DB deploy (admin/API need a server runtime). Public pages remain SSG/ISR, but the deploy target and ops change — plan for an always-on EU DB.
Solo-maintenance of a self-hosted stack: you own Payload version upgrades and Postgres migrations. Mitigate by pinning versions and keeping the migration/seed script in repo.

Authorization correctness is security-critical: a mistake in access-control rules could let an owner edit another's listing. Mitigate with explicit tests of the owner-scoped query constraint before go-live.
Image storage cost/egress can creep with owner uploads; cap upload size/count and serve via Next/Image. EU bucket egress is the main variable cost beyond the ~$0–25/mo baseline.

GDPR paperwork, not just region: ensure a Data Processing Agreement with each provider (DB host, storage, app host) and that ALL three are EU-resident; region selection alone is not sufficient compliance.

Owner UX: Payload's general-purpose admin may feel technical to non-technical shop owners; budget a little time to simplify/relabel their edit screen, or the moderation queue will fill with confused submissions.
Free-tier limits: Neon free (0.5GB/project, 100 CU-hours) is fine at this scale but watch for compute pauses; the $5 Launch tier removes that risk cheaply if needed.

audit.notes: Estimated steady-state cost for the recommended stack: roughly EUR 0-30/month (Neon free or $5 Launch + EU object storage egress + EU app hosting), with ZERO per-owner seat cost for all 96 logins — versus per-seat SaaS (Sanity ~$15/seat, Contentful Team ~$300/mo) which scale badly here. Decision drivers in order: (1) native per-owner row scoping + (2) built-in approve-before-live moderation + (3) lives inside the existing Next app keeping SSG/ISR+SEO intact + (4) no per-seat cost for ~96 owners + (5) full EU/GDPR data sovereignty. Payload wins on all five; Supabase wins on 1, 4, 5 but forces you to build 2 and the entire UI; the Prisma/Auth.js DIY route forces you to build everything; Git-based CMS fails 1 and 2 and regresses ISR; Sanity/Contentful fail 4 (per-seat) and Sanity fails 5 (EU is Enterprise-only). Codebase facts grounding this: 96 businesses, ~40-field typed Business model in /Users/jezza/kamp/src/data/businesses.ts (11,059 lines), imported directly in ~16 files (e.g. /Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/ondernemers/[id]/page.tsx, /Users/jezza/kamp/src/lib/schema.ts, sitemap.ts), all consumed at build time via generateStaticParams. Next 16 on-demand revalidation confirmed available in node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/01-getting-started/09-revalidating.md. Sources: Sanity pricing/EU-Enterprise (nayankyada.com, robotostudio.com); Supabase pricing/RLS/EU (uibakery.io, schematichq.com); Payload MIT/self-host/Next-plugin (luckymedia.dev, nayankyada.com); Neon pricing/EU (vela.simplyblock.io, checkthat.ai); Directus/Strapi/Contentful (capterra, danubedata.ro); Decap/Tina maintenance (luckymedia.dev, github decaporg/tinacms).

audit.area: Auth and owner-portal model for Ondernemers van de Kamp (Next.js 16 static directory)
audit.recommendation:
Use a managed auth provider with magic-link (passwordless email) as the primary sign-in, NOT a hand-rolled JWT/cookie scheme. Concretely: adopt Supabase Auth (magic link + an admin-issued invite flow), backed by Supabase Postgres + Supabase Storage for photos. Keep the public site exactly as it is today (fully static SSG via generateStaticParams over src/data/businesses.ts); the owner portal is a separate, dynamic, non-indexed area (/dashboard, /login, /admin) that never touches the static render path. Publishing is a moderation step: owner edits land in a Postgres "pending changes" table, the admin approves them, and approval writes the canonical record + triggers revalidatePath() so the static page regenerates (ISR) without a full redeploy. This satisfies every brief constraint: secure, low-maintenance, low-cost, EU/GDPR-appropriate, and zero SEO/CWV regression because the public pages stay static.

Why magic-link over passwords: the real flow is "owner emails admin -> admin issues a login." With passwords the admin must generate, transmit, and have the owner rotate a secret — friction and a leak vector for a non-technical shopkeeper audience. With magic links the admin just adds the owner's email to the allowlist (an invite row); the owner clicks "log in," receives a one-time link, and is in. No password to forget, store, or phish. For a ~96-business directory run by a solo operator this is the lowest-support-burden option that is also the most secure default.

Why a provider over DIY: the Next.js 16 auth guide itself recommends a library; rolling your own JWT/jose + cookie + magic-link-token + rate-limiting + email-delivery is exactly the "quickly becomes complex" path it warns against. Supabase gives you magic links, session/refresh handling, an EU (Frankfurt) region for GDPR, Postgres (replacing the read-mostly TS file as the write store), Storage for uploads, and Row Level Security — all on a free/low tier suitable for this scale.

options.name: Supabase Auth (magic link) + Supabase Postgres + Storage — RECOMMENDED
options.pros: One vendor covers auth + DB + image storage; native magic-link + invite-by-email; EU/Frankfurt region for GDPR data residency; Row Level Security enforces 'owner edits only their business' at the database layer (defense in depth beyond app code); generous free tier (~50k MAU, 1GB storage) easily covers 96 owners + 1 admin; SSR helpers documented for Next.js App Router; Storage gives you the local-path photo URLs that BusinessImage/next/image already expect. Replaces the write-side of the TS data file with a real table while the public site can still build from a generated snapshot.
options.cons: Introduces a hosted Postgres dependency (a backend to keep alive) where today there is none; a small data-sync story is needed (DB -> static build); free tier projects pause after ~1 week of inactivity (mitigate with a cron ping or the $25/mo Pro tier).

options.name: Auth.js / NextAuth v5 (magic-link Email provider) + your own DB
options.pros: Free, no auth vendor lock-in, runs in-process; Email provider gives passwordless magic links; flexible roles in the session callback. Good if you specifically want to avoid Supabase.
options.cons: You still must supply and run a database adapter (Postgres/Neon/Turso) AND a separate file/image store AND an SMTP/email sender — you assemble 3 services instead of 1. More wiring, more for a solo operator to maintain. No built-in row-level authorization; all 'owner owns business' checks live in your app code only.

options.name: Clerk (managed, hosted UI, invitations API)
options.pros: Best-in-class DX; built-in Organizations/roles, a first-class 'invitation' API that maps cleanly onto 'admin issues a login,' email magic links, prebuilt sign-in UI; offloads all session/security. Fast to ship.
options.cons: US-headquartered SaaS — GDPR-workable via DPA/SCCs but less clean than an EU-region store for a Dutch civic project; still need a separate DB + image store for the business data and pending-changes/moderation; pricing grows beyond the free MAU tier; another vendor's outage = no logins. Overkill for 97 total users.

options.name: Hand-rolled magic link (jose JWT + cookies()) per the Next.js guide
options.pros: Zero third-party auth dependency; full control; the Next.js 16 guide shows the exact stateless-session primitives (jose HS256, httpOnly/secure/sameSite cookie, Proxy optimistic check, DAL verifySession).
options.cons: You must build and secure: one-time token generation + expiry + single-use invalidation, email delivery, rate limiting/brute-force protection, session rotation, CSRF posture, and a users/sessions store — all yourself. Highest long-term security and maintenance risk; explicitly the path the official docs caution against. Not appropriate for a solo non-specialist operator.

options.name: Static password / shared link / HTTP Basic auth
options.pros: Trivial to set up.
options.cons: Cannot scope an owner to a single business, no per-owner identity, no moderation trail, no GDPR-defensible access control. Disqualified for the stated multi-owner edit-your-own-listing requirement.

decisions.question: Auth method: magic-link/email vs password vs managed provider?
decisions.answer: Passwordless magic link (one-time email link) as the primary method, delivered by a managed provider (Supabase Auth). No passwords. This matches the real flow (admin allowlists the owner's email; owner clicks to log in) and minimizes support burden for non-technical shopkeepers. Optionally allow a returning owner to also receive a 6-digit email OTP as a fallback if a link is mangled by an email client.

decisions.question: How is an owner account linked to one (or more) business records?
decisions.answer: Via a join table, not a column, so multi-business owners are supported from day one. Schema: business_owners(user_id uuid REFERENCES auth.users, business_id text REFERENCES businesses.id, role text default 'owner', created_at). One owner can hold rows for several businesses (e.g. a couple running two shops); one business can have multiple owner contacts. Authorization for any edit = 'does a business_owners row exist for (session.userId, targetBusinessId)?'. With Supabase, encode this as a Row Level Security policy so the database itself rejects cross-business writes even if app code has a bug.

decisions.question: Roles: owner vs admin — where do they live?
decisions.answer: Two roles. Store role on the user/profile: a profiles(user_id, role) table where role is 'admin' or 'owner' (default 'owner'). The admin (the client) is a single hardcoded-by-allowlist admin account. Put the role in the session/JWT claims so Proxy can do an optimistic role check, but ALWAYS re-verify the role server-side in the DAL (verifySession) before any mutation — never trust the cookie alone. Admin role grants: invite owners, view/approve pending changes, edit any business, manage the list. Owner role grants: edit only businesses linked via business_owners, and only into the pending-changes queue (their edits are not live until approved).

decisions.question: How does the admin invite/issue a login?
decisions.answer: Admin opens /admin/owners, enters the owner's email + selects the business(es) to link, and clicks Invite. This (1) creates/looks-up the auth user by email, (2) inserts the business_owners link row(s), and (3) sends an invite email containing a magic link (Supabase admin.inviteUserByEmail or generateLink, called from a server-side Route Handler / Server Action using the service-role key — never client-side). The owner clicks the link, a session is established, and they land on /dashboard scoped to their linked business(es). No password is ever set or transmitted. Revoking access = delete the business_owners row (and optionally disable the user).

decisions.question: Session handling?
decisions.answer: Provider-managed sessions stored in httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax cookies (Supabase's @supabase/ssr handles set/refresh in Server Actions, Route Handlers, and Proxy). If hand-built instead, follow the Next.js 16 guide: jose HS256 JWT in an httpOnly/Secure/SameSite=Lax cookie via the async cookies() API, 7-day expiry with sliding refresh, deleteSession() on logout. Either way the session payload carries only {userId, role} — no PII, no business data. Sessions verified through a single Data Access Layer function verifySession() wrapped in React cache().

decisions.question: Route protection for the owner dashboard (Next.js 16 specifics)?
decisions.answer: Three layers. (1) Optimistic gate in proxy.ts — NOTE: in Next.js 16 'middleware' is renamed to Proxy; create a single proxy.ts at the src/ root with a matcher of ['/dashboard/:path*','/admin/:path*'] that reads ONLY the session cookie (no DB calls — Proxy must stay fast and must not do data fetching) and redirects unauthenticated users to /login. (2) Authoritative check in a DAL verifySession() called inside every protected page/layout's Server Component and at the top of EVERY Server Action and Route Handler (treat Server Actions as public endpoints — the guide is explicit). (3) Resource-level check: before any mutation, confirm a business_owners row links the session user to the target business (or role==='admin'); with Supabase, RLS enforces this at the DB even if a code path forgets. Do auth checks close to the data, not in layouts (layouts don't re-render on client navigation).

decisions.question: How do approved edits reach the static public site without losing SSG/SEO?
decisions.answer: Owner edits write to a business_edits(business_id, payload jsonb, status 'pending', submitted_by, submitted_at) table — nothing public changes yet. Admin reviews diffs + uploaded photos in /admin and clicks Approve, which merges the payload into the canonical businesses table and calls revalidatePath(`/ondernemers/${id}`) (plus the relevant /categorie/[slug] and home) from the Server Action. The public route /ondernemers/[id] keeps generateStaticParams + a moderate `export const revalidate` (or pure on-demand ISR), so it stays static/CDN-cached and re-renders only on approval. JSON-LD, sitemap, and llms.txt continue to read the same canonical data, so structured data and Core Web Vitals are unaffected. (Migration note: the canonical store moves from the src/data/businesses.ts file to the businesses table; keep a build-time export of the table to a snapshot if you want to retain a file-based fallback, but the live read path for SSG should be the DB.)

decisions.question: Photo upload / moderation?
decisions.answer: Owners upload to a private Supabase Storage bucket (or an 'incoming' prefix) via a server-validated upload (MIME allowlist: jpeg/png/webp; size cap ~5MB; strip EXIF/GPS). Uploaded photos attach to the pending business_edit and are shown to the admin for approval — mirroring today's 'photos only after owner akkoord' policy but reversed to admin-gate. On approval the image is moved/copied to the public path the site already consumes (a /-prefixed URL) so next/image optimizes it exactly as the current BusinessImage component expects; imageStatus flips to 'approved'. Public imageCandidateUrls remain stored-but-never-rendered, unchanged.

steps: Provision Supabase (EU/Frankfurt region). Create tables: profiles(user_id, role), business_owners(user_id, business_id, role), business_edits(id, business_id, payload jsonb, status, submitted_by, submitted_at, reviewed_by, reviewed_at), and a businesses table seeded by importing the current src/data/businesses.ts. Enable Row Level Security on all of them.

Migrate the data read path: generate the public SSG data from the businesses table (build-time fetch or a committed snapshot), keeping src/data/businesses.ts's Business type as the shared TypeScript contract. Verify the static build, JSON-LD, sitemap, and llms.txt are byte-for-byte equivalent before going further.

Add @supabase/supabase-js + @supabase/ssr. Create src/lib/supabase/{server,client}.ts wrappers and src/lib/dal.ts exporting cache()-wrapped verifySession() and a requireBusinessAccess(businessId) helper. Add env vars: NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, and SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (server-only, used only in Route Handlers/Server Actions for admin invites).

Create src/proxy.ts (Next.js 16 renamed middleware -> Proxy) with matcher ['/dashboard/:path*','/admin/:path*'], doing an optimistic cookie-only session check that redirects to /login. Do NOT put DB calls or the authoritative role check here.
Build /login (magic-link request form -> Server Action that calls signInWithOtp) and the magic-link callback Route Handler that exchanges the code for a session cookie and redirects to /dashboard.

Build /dashboard: a Server Component that calls verifySession(), lists the businesses linked to the user via business_owners, and an edit form per business (descriptions, hours via the existing DayHours structure, phone/website/socials/price/tags, photo upload). The submit Server Action re-runs verifySession() + requireBusinessAccess() then inserts a business_edits row (status 'pending'); it never writes the live record.

Build /admin (role==='admin' only, verified in the DAL): an Owners screen (invite by email + link to business, calling admin.inviteUserByEmail server-side) and a Moderation queue showing pending business_edits diffs + uploaded photos with Approve/Reject. Approve merges payload into businesses, promotes the photo to the public path, and calls revalidatePath for the affected /ondernemers/[id], /categorie/[slug], and / routes.

Add a robots/noindex header and 'X-Robots-Tag: noindex' (or a route-segment robots) for /dashboard, /admin, /login so the portal never gets indexed. Add a small cron (Vercel cron or Supabase pg_cron) ping to keep a free-tier Supabase project from pausing.

Replace the /aanmelden mailto form: keep public self-signup as a 'request access' that creates a pending invite for the admin to approve (so the admin still gates who gets a login), preserving the current 'we nemen contact op' tone. Existing AanmeldenForm/OwnerSubmitCta become the entry point into this flow.

Security pass: confirm RLS rejects cross-business writes (test as a non-owner), confirm every Server Action/Route Handler calls verifySession() (the guide treats them as public endpoints), validate upload MIME/size + strip EXIF, set the session cookie httpOnly/Secure/SameSite=Lax, and add a short GDPR/privacy note covering owner email + uploaded photos with a deletion path (delete user + business_owners + their edits).
risks: Architectural shift: today there is NO backend. Adopting auth introduces a hosted DB + storage that must be kept alive, backed up, and (on Supabase free tier) pinged to avoid pausing. This is the single biggest change to the project's 'static, low-maintenance' character — budget for it explicitly.

Next.js 16 gotcha: 'middleware' is renamed to Proxy (proxy.ts). Proxy runs before render and must NOT do data fetching or DB calls — it is for optimistic cookie checks only. Putting auth DB lookups there will hurt performance and can block static assets if the matcher is too broad (exclude api/_next/static/_next/image/public assets).

Auth-in-layout trap: the docs warn layouts don't re-render on client-side navigation, so a single layout-level auth check is insufficient. Checks must live in the DAL and run inside each page, Server Action, and Route Handler.

Server Actions are public endpoints: every mutation must independently call verifySession() + the resource-ownership check. A client-side hidden button is not access control. Forgetting this on one action is a full authz bypass — RLS on Supabase is the recommended backstop.

SEO/CWV regression risk if the public pages are accidentally made dynamic. Keep /ondernemers/[id] static with generateStaticParams and use on-demand revalidatePath on approval; never gate public routes behind Proxy/session.

Data-store migration risk: moving the canonical source from src/data/businesses.ts to a table can desync the build. Mitigate by keeping the Business type as the contract and snapshotting the table to a build-time file until confidence is high.

Image-upload abuse / privacy: uploaded photos can carry EXIF GPS or be oversized/malicious. Enforce server-side MIME+size validation and EXIF stripping; keep the admin-approval gate (matches the existing 'photos only after akkoord' policy).

GDPR: storing owner emails + photos creates a controller obligation. Use an EU region, a DPA with the provider, a privacy statement, and a concrete deletion path (delete user, business_owners links, and their pending edits).

@supabase/ssr
zod (server-side validation of edit payloads, per the Next.js auth guide)
audit.notes: Current-system audit (verified by reading the repo): the site is 100% static — all data is the typed array in /Users/jezza/kamp/src/data/businesses.ts (the Business interface, ~50 optional fields incl. structured hours: DayHours[]), consumed at build by generateStaticParams in /Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/ondernemers/[id]/page.tsx and /Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/categorie/[slug]/page.tsx, plus /Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/sitemap.ts and /Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/llms.txt/route.ts (force-static). There are NO API routes, NO middleware/proxy, NO auth, NO DB today. Owner onboarding is purely a client-side mailto form: /Users/jezza/kamp/src/components/AanmeldenForm.tsx (and OwnerSubmitCta.tsx). next.config.ts is empty (no images.remotePatterns), and /Users/jezza/kamp/src/components/BusinessImage.tsx uses next/image only for local '/'-prefixed owner photos and a raw for external URLs — so approved owner uploads should be written to a local/public '/' path to get optimization, which the recommended Storage->public-path-on-approval step does. Next.js 16 facts confirmed from /Users/jezza/kamp/node_modules/next/dist/docs/: (1) Middleware is renamed to Proxy — file is proxy.ts at the src/ root, single file per project, runs on Node.js runtime, optimistic cookie checks only, must exclude static assets via matcher. (2) The official authentication guide explicitly recommends an auth library over DIY and lists Supabase, Clerk, Auth.js/NextAuth among supported options. (3) cookies() is async (await cookies()). (4) revalidatePath works in Server Actions and Route Handlers (NOT in Client Components or Proxy) — this is the mechanism to push approved edits into the static pages without a redeploy. (5) Recommended cookie options: httpOnly, Secure, SameSite, Max-Age/Expires, Path. (6) DAL pattern with cache()-wrapped verifySession() is the prescribed authorization centralization. Relevant files for implementation: /Users/jezza/kamp/src/data/businesses.ts (type contract + seed), /Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/ondernemers/[id]/page.tsx (revalidate target), /Users/jezza/kamp/src/components/BusinessImage.tsx (photo path contract), /Users/jezza/kamp/src/components/AanmeldenForm.tsx + /Users/jezza/kamp/src/components/OwnerSubmitCta.tsx (entry point to convert into request-access), /Users/jezza/kamp/next.config.ts (add images config if Storage URLs are ever served remotely). New files to create: src/proxy.ts, src/lib/dal.ts, src/lib/supabase/{server,client}.ts, src/app/login, src/app/dashboard, src/app/admin.

audit.area: Image upload & storage for owner-managed business photos (hero + gallery), with moderation, for the Ondernemers van de Kamp Next.js 16 SSG directory
audit.recommendation: Use Supabase for the whole feature: Supabase Storage for files, Supabase Postgres for the draft/moderation state + the published URL, and Supabase Auth for owner/admin logins. Pair it with Next.js 16 remote image optimization (next/image + images.remotePatterns) and convert /ondernemers/[id] and /kaart to ISR with on-demand revalidation so approved photos go live without a redeploy. WHY ONE PROVIDER, NOT A POINT TOOL: The current site is pure SSG with ALL data in a typed TS file (src/data/businesses.ts, 98 records) and NO database, NO auth, NO API routes, NO build-time fetch (confirmed: generateStaticParams reads the array directly; no env files exist). Image upload is therefore not just a "where do the bytes go" question — it requires (1) somewhere to store the bytes, (2) somewhere to store who-uploaded-what + pending/approved moderation state + the resulting URL keyed to the business, and (3) a way to push the approved URL onto a static page without a manual redeploy. Vercel Blob, Cloudinary, and UploadThing only solve (1) (and Cloudinary also does transforms); they do NOT give you the auth + moderation-state DB, so you'd have to bolt on a second system anyway. Supabase delivers storage + Postgres + Auth + Row Level Security in one EU-region project, which is the smallest RELIABLE setup for a solo/committee operator. Cloudinary is the runner-up ONLY if you want zero image-pipeline code (URL-based transforms) and are willing to run auth + the moderation DB separately. DATA MODEL (do NOT mutate businesses.ts at runtime — keep it as the SSG seed/fallback): Add a Postgres table, e.g. `business_media` { id, business_id (matches Business.id), kind: 'hero' | 'gallery', storage_path, public_url, width, height, blurhash, sort_order, status: 'pending' | 'approved' | 'rejected' | 'replaced', uploaded_by, created_at, reviewed_by, reviewed_at }, plus `business_overrides` for the editable text/hours/socials fields with the same pending/approved status, and `owner_business` { user_id, business_id } to scope edits. At render time the page reads APPROVED rows for that business_id and falls back to the existing businesses.ts imageUrl/gallery when none exist — so the site degrades gracefully and the 98 existing records keep working untouched. HOW THE EXISTING imageStatus/permissionStatus MODEL MAPS (reuse it, don't reinvent): The type already has imageStatus ('placeholder' | 'shopfront_needed' | 'owner_photo_needed' | 'approved'), permissionStatus ('placeholder_only' | 'public_business_info' | 'owner_photo_needed' | 'owner_approved'), verificationStatus ('owner_approved'), and an imageCandidateUrl that the project rule says is 'stored but NEVER rendered'. Mirror this exactly in the DB status enum: a new owner upload lands as imageStatus='owner_photo_needed' / permissionStatus='owner_photo_needed' (pending) and is rendered ONLY after an admin sets status='approved' / permissionStatus='owner_approved' — which is the same NEVER-render-until-approved invariant that imageCandidateUrl already enforces. This means the moderation gate is a continuation of an existing rule, not a new policy. UPLOAD UI: A protected /beheer (owner dashboard) route behind Supabase Auth. Drag-drop + file picker (a small client component; you already use 'use client' components like AanmeldenForm and have lucide-react Camera/Upload icons). Show current approved photo, a 'pending review' banner for anything awaiting moderation, hero vs gallery slots, drag-to-reorder gallery, and remove. Mobile-first (owners will use phones). Keep the existing /aanmelden mailto flow as the public 'request a login' entry point — the admin issues the login from the dashboard. VALIDATION: Enforce client-side AND server-side (in a Server Action / route handler). Accept image/jpeg, image/png, image/webp, image/avif only; reject HEIC by asking to re-export or transcode server-side; max ~10 MB per file; sniff real magic bytes (not just the extension/declared MIME); cap gallery count (e.g. 8). Strip EXIF/GPS on ingest (privacy + GDPR). Use a Supabase Storage RLS policy so an owner can only write to their own business_id prefix. RESIZING/OPTIMIZATION + CWV: Owners upload large phone photos; do NOT serve those raw. On upload, generate a normalized master (e.g. resize to max 2000px, re-encode to WebP, strip EXIF) using sharp in the Server Action, store that in Supabase Storage, and record width/height + a tiny blurhash/blurDataURL. Then let next/image do per-device responsive sizing/format at request time: add the Supabase Storage hostname to images.remotePatterns in next.config.ts (currently empty — this is required for next/image to optimize remote URLs in Next 16). Always render via with the stored width/height/blurDataURL and a correct sizes prop to keep CLS=0 and good LCP. BusinessImage.tsx already implements local-photo → external → placeholder precedence with object-cover/contain and imageFit — extend branch 1 to also treat approved Supabase URLs as optimizable next/image sources (today branch 2 uses a raw for external URLs, which is NOT optimized — that is exactly the regression to avoid for owner photos). MODERATION BEFORE GOING LIVE: Admin view in /beheer lists pending business_media + business_overrides rows with before/after preview and Approve/Reject buttons (writing reviewed_by/reviewed_at). Nothing pending is ever rendered publicly (RLS + the render-only-approved query enforce this). On Approve, the Server Action flips status to 'approved' and triggers on-demand revalidation. PUBLISH WITHOUT REDEPLOY (Next 16 specifics, verified against node_modules/next/dist/docs): Convert /ondernemers/[id] and /kaart from build-time-only SSG to ISR by reading approved media from Supabase in the page (keep generateStaticParams for prebuild + SEO). On Approve, call revalidatePath('/ondernemers/[id]','page') and revalidatePath('/kaart') from the Server Action. NOTE the Next 16 breaking changes that bite here: revalidateTag now REQUIRES a second cacheLife argument (e.g. revalidateTag('biz-123','max')) — the single-arg form is a TS error; for immediate (non-stale-while-revalidate) expiry use updateTag in a Server Action; params/searchParams are async (await them). JSON-LD/schema and sitemap keep working because they read the same merged data source. This keeps SSG/SEO/structured data intact while letting an approved photo appear within seconds. SECURITY/GDPR (EU/NL): Create the Supabase project in an EU region (eu-central-1 Frankfurt or eu-west-1 Ireland) so primary data AND backups stay in the EU; sign Supabase's DPA. Note Supabase is a US (Delaware) entity, so EU region gives data RESIDENCY but not full sovereignty — fine for a public business directory with owner-consented photos, and better than Vercel Blob/Cloudinary/UploadThing whose default residency is less explicit. Strip EXIF/GPS, store an explicit consent timestamp (the /aanmelden form already collects a permission checkbox — persist that), and keep the 'owner photos only with permission' rule the memory already mandates. SMALLEST RELIABLE SETUP (cost): Supabase Free tier (1 GB file storage, 500 MB DB, auth included) comfortably covers ~96 businesses × a few photos each; upgrade to Pro ($25/mo) only if storage/bandwidth grows. next/image optimization runs on your existing Vercel deploy at no added service. Total new monthly cost: $0 to start, ~$25 at scale. One provider to operate, which matches the solo/small-committee constraint.

options.name: Supabase (Storage + Postgres + Auth) — RECOMMENDED
options.pros: One EU-region provider solves ALL three gaps the static site has: file storage, the moderation-state/URL database, AND owner+admin auth with Row Level Security to scope edits per business. Free tier (1 GB storage, 500 MB Postgres, auth) covers ~96 businesses; Pro is $25/mo. Explicit EU region (Frankfurt/Ireland) keeps primary data + backups in the EU; DPA available. Postgres is the natural home for pending/approved moderation rows mapped onto the existing imageStatus/permissionStatus enums. Lowest operational surface for a solo operator.
options.cons: No built-in image transformation (Storage is plain S3-like object storage) — you do resize/WebP/EXIF-strip yourself with sharp in a Server Action (one small function). Supabase is a US (Delaware) corp, so EU region = residency, not full sovereignty. You write the auth/dashboard UI (but it's standard Supabase Auth).

options.name: Cloudinary (managed media pipeline)
options.pros: Best-in-class image handling: URL-based on-the-fly resize/crop/format(auto WebP/AVIF)/quality + global CDN, so you write almost zero image-processing code and can even bypass next/image optimization. Free tier ~25 credits/mo (~25 GB storage or 25k transforms). Mature, reliable.
options.cons: Solves ONLY storage+transforms — you still need a SEPARATE auth system and a SEPARATE database for owner/admin login and the pending/approved moderation state, so it does NOT reduce the number of systems for THIS feature; it adds one. Free-tier overage behavior can suspend the account (assets go inaccessible) rather than bill. EU data residency not a highlighted default — needs DPA + region confirmation. Most expensive paid tiers ($89+/mo).

options.name: Vercel Blob (storage on the existing platform)
options.pros: Already on Vercel, so zero new vendor for storage; simple put()/del() SDK, durable, generous Hobby allotment. EU region selectable for the blob store. Pairs cleanly with next/image via remotePatterns.
options.cons: Storage ONLY — no auth, no database, no image transformation, no moderation primitives. You'd still add a separate DB (for moderation state + URLs) and a separate auth system, so it leaves two of the three gaps unsolved. Vercel is US-incorporated / CLOUD Act exposure; data-residency controls still maturing. You implement all resizing/optimization yourself.

options.name: UploadThing (DX-focused upload service)
options.pros: Very fast to wire up upload UI + signed uploads in a Next.js app; reasonable free storage (~2–10 GB depending on source) and cheap $10/mo for 100 GB.
options.cons: Storage/upload ONLY — no image transformation, no database for moderation state, no auth/RLS to scope edits per owner. EU data residency / GDPR posture not clearly documented (no explicit EU region guarantee found) — weakest fit for an NL/GDPR project. Smallest, youngest vendor of the four; leaves the same two gaps as Vercel Blob plus weaker compliance story.

decisions.question: Where is the photo URL stored on the business record?
decisions.answer: NOT in businesses.ts (that stays the immutable SSG seed/fallback). Approved URLs live in a new Postgres `business_media` table keyed by business_id (kind: hero|gallery, status, public_url, width/height/blurhash). Pages merge approved rows over the businesses.ts imageUrl/gallery fallback at render time.

decisions.question: How does the existing imageStatus / permissionStatus / imageCandidateUrl model fit?
decisions.answer: Reuse it directly. New uploads = imageStatus 'owner_photo_needed' + permissionStatus 'owner_photo_needed' (pending). Admin approval flips to imageStatus 'approved' + permissionStatus 'owner_approved' + verificationStatus 'owner_approved'. This is the SAME never-render-until-approved invariant the project already enforces for imageCandidateUrl ('stored but never rendered'), so the moderation gate continues an existing rule rather than adding a new policy.

decisions.question: How do approved photos stay fast / not regress Core Web Vitals?
decisions.answer: Two layers: (1) on upload, normalize with sharp to max ~2000px WebP, strip EXIF, record width/height + blurDataURL; (2) render via next/image (add the Supabase host to images.remotePatterns — currently empty) with fill + sizes + blur placeholder for CLS=0 and optimized LCP. Today BusinessImage.tsx serves EXTERNAL urls via a raw unoptimized (branch 2) — owner photos must go through the next/image path (branch 1) to avoid that regression.

decisions.question: How does an approved photo go live on a STATIC page without a redeploy?
decisions.answer: Convert /ondernemers/[id] and /kaart to ISR (keep generateStaticParams for prebuild+SEO) reading approved media from Supabase. On Approve, the Server Action calls revalidatePath('/ondernemers/[id]','page') + revalidatePath('/kaart'). Next 16 caveats: revalidateTag now needs a cacheLife second arg, use updateTag for immediate expiry, await params/searchParams. JSON-LD + sitemap read the same merged source so structured data stays intact.

decisions.question: What is moderated and by whom?
decisions.answer: Both photos AND text/hours/socials edits land as pending rows (business_media / business_overrides). Owners (Supabase Auth, RLS-scoped to their own business_id) can only create pending changes. Admin reviews before/after in /beheer and Approves/Rejects; only approved rows are ever queried for public render. Admin also issues/invites owner logins and manages the full list.

decisions.question: Is it GDPR/EU appropriate?
decisions.answer: Yes with care: create the Supabase project in an EU region (Frankfurt eu-central-1 or Ireland eu-west-1) so primary data + backups stay in the EU, sign the DPA, strip EXIF/GPS on ingest, and persist the consent timestamp the /aanmelden permission checkbox already collects. Supabase being a US corp means residency not full sovereignty — acceptable for a public, consent-based business directory and stronger than the other three options' default posture.

packages: @supabase/supabase-js (storage + DB + auth client)
@supabase/ssr (auth in Next.js App Router server components / route handlers / middleware)
sharp (server-side resize, WebP/AVIF re-encode, EXIF strip on upload)
blurhash or built-in plaiceholder (generate blurDataURL for next/image placeholder)
file-type (server-side magic-byte MIME sniffing for validation)
steps: Provision a Supabase project in an EU region (Frankfurt/Ireland); sign the DPA. Add SUPABASE_URL + anon + service-role keys to a new .env.local (none exist today).

Create tables: business_media, business_overrides, owner_business; enable Row Level Security so an owner can only read/write rows for their own business_id, and only admins can flip status to 'approved'. Add a Storage bucket with a per-business path prefix policy.
Add the Supabase Storage hostname to images.remotePatterns in next.config.ts (currently empty {}) so next/image optimizes approved remote photos.

Build a data-access layer that, for a given business_id, returns merged data = approved business_media/overrides over the businesses.ts fallback. Use this in /ondernemers/[id], /kaart, sitemap, and schema so SEO/JSON-LD read the same source.

Convert /ondernemers/[id] and /kaart to ISR (keep generateStaticParams). Extend BusinessImage.tsx so approved Supabase URLs render through the next/image (branch 1) path with width/height/blurDataURL, not the raw external branch.
Implement Supabase Auth + a protected /beheer route via @supabase/ssr + middleware. Owners land on their own listing; an admin role sees all + an invite/issue-login action.
Build the owner upload UI (mobile-first drag-drop, hero + gallery slots, reorder, remove, 'pending review' banner). Reuse lucide-react Camera/Upload icons and the existing 'use client' form patterns.

Implement a Server Action / route handler for upload: validate MIME by magic bytes + size cap + count cap, strip EXIF, resize to WebP master with sharp, compute width/height + blurDataURL, store in Supabase Storage, insert a 'pending' business_media row mapped to imageStatus='owner_photo_needed'.

Build the admin moderation view in /beheer: before/after preview + Approve/Reject. On Approve, flip status to 'approved' (imageStatus='approved', permissionStatus='owner_approved') and call revalidatePath for the business page + /kaart (mind Next 16 revalidateTag cacheLife arg / updateTag).

Persist consent timestamp (from the /aanmelden permission checkbox) and verify end-to-end: upload → pending (not public) → approve → appears on the live page within seconds via ISR, with CLS=0 and optimized LCP confirmed.
Keep /aanmelden mailto as the public 'request a login' funnel; the admin issues the login from /beheer.
risks: Runtime data reads break the current pure-SSG model: /ondernemers/[id] and /kaart must move to ISR. If misconfigured, you regress to all-dynamic (slower, worse CWV/SEO). Mitigate by keeping generateStaticParams + reading approved media with proper cache tags and on-demand revalidation only.

Next.js 16 breaking changes will bite if copied from older examples: async params/searchParams, revalidateTag REQUIRES a cacheLife second arg (single-arg = TS error), updateTag for immediate expiry. Follow node_modules/next/dist/docs, not training-data patterns (per AGENTS.md).

Unoptimized image regression: today external imageUrls render via a raw (BusinessImage branch 2). If owner photos take that path they skip next/image optimization → LCP/bandwidth regression. Route approved photos through the next/image path and set remotePatterns.

Moderation bypass: if a page ever queries non-approved rows, pending/un-consented photos leak publicly — violating the project's 'photos only after owner approval' rule. Enforce at BOTH the query layer and Storage RLS.

GDPR: raw phone photos carry EXIF GPS; failing to strip on ingest is a privacy leak. Always strip EXIF and store explicit consent. Supabase US-corp = residency not sovereignty; acceptable here but document it.

Self-uploaded image quality varies (orientation, aspect, huge files): handle EXIF orientation in sharp, enforce size/count caps, and keep the generative placeholder as the last-resort fallback so layouts never break.

Cost/lock-in creep: starting free is fine, but bandwidth from many large galleries can push past free tier. Cap gallery counts and serve only optimized derivatives; monitor Supabase usage before it forces an unplanned upgrade.
audit.notes: Audit confirms the constraints driving this recommendation. Key files: src/data/businesses.ts (Business type lines 87–183; imageUrl/imageStatus/permissionStatus/imageCandidateUrl/gallery already defined; 98 records; gallery field exists but is NOT rendered anywhere yet), src/components/BusinessImage.tsx (3-tier precedence; branch 1 = optimized next/image for local '/' paths, branch 2 = UNOPTIMIZED raw for external https urls — the regression to avoid), src/app/ondernemers/[id]/page.tsx (build-time SSG via generateStaticParams; metadata only uses local '/' images as OG), src/components/AanmeldenForm.tsx (today just builds a mailto with a permission checkbox; no upload), next.config.ts (empty — images.remotePatterns must be added). Confirmed absent: any next-auth/supabase/prisma/drizzle/blob/cloudinary/uploadthing dependency, any route handler (POST/GET), any cookies()/auth, any .env file. Next 16 docs consulted: 01-getting-started/12-images.md (remotePatterns required for remote next/image), upgrading/version-16.md (async params; revalidateTag needs cacheLife arg; updateTag for immediate expiry), revalidatePath.md, ISR guide. Provider posture (web-researched June 2026): Cloudinary free ~25 credits/mo with suspend-on-overage; Supabase free 1 GB storage/500 MB DB + EU region selectable (Delaware corp); UploadThing storage-only, weak documented EU/GDPR story; Vercel Blob storage-only on existing platform, US CLOUD Act exposure. Bottom line: the static-data architecture (no DB/auth) is what makes Supabase the smallest reliable choice — the other three solve only the storage slice and force a second system for auth + moderation state.

audit.area: Data model & migration from static src/data/businesses.ts to a backend store, plus the SEO-safe read path (schema, draft/published + change-requests, one-time import, ISR/generateStaticParams/revalidate)
audit.recommendation: Use Supabase (managed Postgres in the EU region) as the single backend for data, auth, and image storage. Model each listing as one `businesses` row that mirrors the existing Business type almost field-for-field, keeping the editorial/structured pieces (hours, perfectFor, keyFacts, specialties, tags, sourceUrls, socials) as JSONB / text[] so the TS types and JSON-LD builders stay unchanged. Implement draft-vs-published as TWO column sets on the same row (published_* is what the public site reads; draft_* is the owner's working copy) plus a separate `change_requests` audit/moderation table; photos go through a `media` table with a `pending|approved` status so nothing reaches the public bundle un-moderated. Do the migration as a ONE-TIME, idempotent Node import script that imports the real `businesses` array from the TS file and upserts by `id` into both published_* and draft_* (keep businesses.ts in git as the seed/backup, then stop importing it in app code). For the read path, keep full SSG: a cached data layer (`unstable_cache` tagged `businesses` / `business:{id}`) feeds `generateStaticParams` (returns the full active set, exactly as today) and the page bodies; set a long `revalidate` (e.g. 3600s) as a safety net and trigger instant on-publish freshness by calling `revalidatePath('/ondernemers/{id}')` + `revalidateTag('businesses')` from the admin "approve" Server Action (or a secured webhook). Structured data and generateStaticParams do NOT change shape — they read the same Business object, now sourced from Postgres instead of the literal array.

options.name: Supabase (managed Postgres + Auth + Storage), EU region
options.pros: One vendor covers all three new needs: relational data, owner/admin auth (magic-link / invite, RLS), and image storage with CDN + transforms. Postgres JSONB stores hours/perfectFor/keyFacts verbatim so the Business TS type and every JSON-LD builder stay identical. Row-Level Security lets an owner edit only their own draft row. Free/low tier fits a 96-row solo-operated directory; EU project = GDPR-appropriate data residency. Pairs naturally with Vercel ISR + revalidateTag.
options.cons: Another managed service to operate; RLS has a learning curve; Storage image transforms are a paid add-on (or use next/image instead).

options.name: Postgres (Neon/Vercel Postgres) + Drizzle/Prisma, auth via Auth.js, images via Vercel Blob/UploadThing
options.pros: Maximum control and a clean typed ORM that can generate types matching the Business interface; Neon EU region for residency; decouples DB from auth/storage so each piece is swappable.
options.cons: You assemble 3+ services (DB + Auth.js + blob storage) and write the moderation/RLS logic yourself — more glue code and more to maintain for a one-person committee. Higher implementation cost than Supabase for the same outcome.

options.name: Headless CMS with editorial workflow (Sanity / Payload / Strapi)
options.pros: Draft-vs-publish, roles, and media moderation are built-in product features — least custom code for the workflow itself. Sanity/Payload give a polished editing UI for the admin out of the box.
options.cons: Per-business owner logins scoped to a single document is awkward in most CMSes (their roles are coarse); mapping ~50 fields incl. structured hours into the CMS schema is fiddly; vendor lock-in / pricing creep; for owners editing only their own listing, a custom Supabase-backed form is simpler and cheaper than bending a CMS's role model.

options.name: Keep businesses.ts as the store; admin edits via Git/PR (Git-as-CMS)
options.pros: Zero new infrastructure; build stays a pure static export; full version history for free.
options.cons: Does not meet the goal: business owners cannot self-serve edits or upload photos without a Git workflow, there is no per-owner auth, and image upload/moderation is impossible. Rebuild-on-every-edit doesn't scale to owner-driven changes.

packages: @supabase/supabase-js (server + admin clients for read/write and the import script)
@supabase/ssr (cookie-based auth for the owner/admin App Router pages)
zod (validate owner-submitted fields server-side before writing draft_* — descriptions, hours shape, urls, price)
tsx (run the one-time import + any data scripts that import the .ts businesses file directly)
Optional: drizzle-orm + drizzle-kit (typed schema + migrations if you prefer SQL-first over Supabase Studio); skip if using Supabase migrations/SQL directly
steps: Provision a Supabase project in an EU region (e.g. eu-central). Add env vars to Vercel: SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (server-only), plus a REVALIDATE_SECRET for the webhook/Server Action.

Create the schema (SQL migration). Table `businesses`: id text PK (= current id, also the route param and de-facto slug), plus a flat set of scalar columns (name, category, subcategory, address, postal_code, city, street_segment, lat, lng, geo_confidence, status, price_range, price_level, schema_type, image_url, image_fit, image_status, permission_status, verification_status, inclusion_status, featured, sort_order, updated_at, last_checked) and JSONB/array columns for the structured pieces (hours jsonb = DayHours[], perfect_for text[], key_facts jsonb, specialties text[], tags text[], good_for text[], dietary_tags text[], highlights text[], source_urls text[], socials jsonb, short_description, long_description, owner_or_story_notes, accessibility_notes, payment_notes, hours_note). Mirror EVERY field so the TS Business type is unchanged.

Implement draft-vs-published WITHOUT duplicating 50 columns: store the public copy in a `published jsonb` (a full serialized Business) and the owner working copy in a `draft jsonb`, keyed by id, with `status enum('draft_pending_review','published')`, `owner_user_id uuid null`, `published_at`, `draft_updated_at`. Public reads deserialize `published`; owner edits write `draft`; admin approval copies draft -> published. (Keep the scalar columns above only for the ones you need to query/sort/filter — id, category, status, featured, sort_order, street_segment, lat/lng — to avoid full-JSON scans; everything else lives in the JSONB blobs.)

Create `change_requests` (id, business_id FK, owner_user_id, submitted_at, reviewed_at, reviewer, decision enum('pending','approved','rejected'), diff jsonb of changed fields, note) for the moderation queue and audit trail; and `media` (id, business_id FK, storage_path, kind enum('cover','gallery'), status enum('pending','approved','rejected'), uploaded_by, created_at, alt). A Supabase Storage bucket holds uploads; only `approved` media URLs are ever surfaced to the public read layer.

Set Row-Level Security: owners (auth.uid() = owner_user_id) can SELECT/UPDATE only their own `draft` and INSERT their own `media`/`change_requests`; they can never write `published`. Admin uses the service-role key (server-only) to read everything and to approve. Public read uses anon key restricted to `status='published'` rows / approved media, or — simpler and faster — the Next server reads via service role inside a cached function and never exposes the DB to the browser.

Write the one-time import script `scripts/import-businesses.ts` (run with tsx): `import { businesses } from '../src/data/businesses'`, then for each record upsert one row by id where `published = record` AND `draft = record` AND status='published', owner_user_id=null. Make it idempotent (ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE) so it can be re-run safely; log inserted/updated/skipped counts; assert count === 98 at the end. Keep businesses.ts committed as the seed/backup, but after import stop importing it from app/page code.

Build a typed data-access layer `src/lib/db.ts`: `getPublishedBusinesses()` and `getPublishedBusiness(id)` that query Supabase, deserialize `published` jsonb into the existing `Business` type (a thin runtime cast / zod parse), and wrap in `unstable_cache(fn, ['businesses'], { tags: ['businesses', `business:${id}`] })`. This is the ONLY change to the read path — every consumer keeps importing the Business type and calling these helpers instead of the literal array.

Update the read path with minimal diffs: in `/ondernemers/[id]/page.tsx`, `generateStaticParams` calls `getPublishedBusinesses()` and returns the same `{ id }[]` (still SSG, full set); the page body and `generateMetadata` call `getPublishedBusiness(id)`. Do the same swap in `sitemap.ts`, `llms.txt/route.ts`, `categorie/[slug]/page.tsx`, `page.tsx`, and the components that import the array (FeaturedHorizontal, WandelShowcase, SeoIntro, OwnerSubmitCta, kaart, over-de-kamp, cadeaukaart, praktisch). The JSON-LD builders in src/lib/schema.ts and hours.ts are UNTOUCHED — they receive the same Business object, so OpeningHoursSpecification, LocalBusiness subtype, founder, breadcrumbs and FAQ output are byte-identical.

Add `export const revalidate = 3600` to the dynamic/list routes as a stale-time safety net (NOT Cache Components — cacheComponents is off, so the classic model applies and generateStaticParams may still return the full array). Pages stay fully prerendered at build; ISR only refreshes them in the background.

Wire instant on-publish freshness: the admin 'Approve' Server Action (or a Supabase DB webhook -> `/api/revalidate?secret=...` route handler) calls `revalidatePath('/ondernemers/${id}')`, `revalidatePath('/categorie/${slug}')`, `revalidatePath('/')`, `revalidatePath('/kaart')`, and `revalidateTag('businesses')` so an approved change goes live within seconds without a full redeploy. Reject/edit actions only touch draft, never revalidate.

Verify no SEO regression: run `next build` and diff the emitted JSON-LD and the prerendered HTML for a sample of /ondernemers/[id] pages before vs after the swap (they should match); confirm the sitemap still lists all active businesses and generateStaticParams still prerenders 96 pages at build.
risks: Read-path regression: if a page accidentally becomes dynamic (e.g. reading cookies/headers, or a fetch with no-store/revalidate:0 in the request path), it drops out of SSG and hurts CWV/SEO. Keep all public reads inside the cached, request-agnostic db layer; never read auth/cookies in public routes.

JSON-LD drift: serializing/deserializing `published` jsonb must round-trip the Business type exactly (esp. hours DayHours[] and optional booleans like acceptsReservations) or OpeningHoursSpecification / schema fields silently disappear. Add a zod schema that mirrors Business and parse on read; snapshot-test the JSON-LD output against current build.

Storage egress / image policy: external imageUrl (most listings today) bypass next/image; owner-uploaded photos in Supabase Storage need next.config images.remotePatterns for the Supabase host AND must be moderated (status='approved') before the public read returns them — otherwise un-approved photos leak into the bundle.

Two-instance ISR / on-demand revalidation only invalidates the instance that receives the call when self-hosting; on Vercel this is handled, but if ever self-hosted you need a shared cache handler. Document the assumption (Vercel).

Service-role key exposure: the admin/import path uses the service-role key which bypasses RLS — it must stay server-only (never NEXT_PUBLIC_, never shipped to client components). A leak = full write access.

Idempotency of the import: re-running the script must not create duplicates or wipe owner drafts that already diverged from published. Guard with ON CONFLICT and only overwrite draft when owner_user_id IS NULL (i.e. not yet claimed by an owner).
Stale businesses.ts: after migration, businesses.ts is a frozen seed. If anyone keeps editing it expecting changes to show, they won't. Add a comment/README note that the DB is now source of truth.

slug vs id: routes use [id] and there is no populated slug; if owners are ever allowed to change a name-derived slug, that breaks canonical URLs and generateStaticParams. Keep id immutable as the route key.

decisions.question: Relational table-per-field vs JSONB for the ~50-field Business (esp. structured hours)?
decisions.answer: JSONB + a few promoted scalar columns. Store the full Business as `published`/`draft` jsonb so the existing TS type and JSON-LD builders need zero changes; promote only the columns you query/sort/filter on (id, category, status, featured, sort_order, street_segment, lat, lng). Avoids a 7-row-per-business hours table and keeps the import a near-direct serialization.

decisions.question: How to model draft-vs-published?
decisions.answer: Single row per business with `published` and `draft` JSONB columns + a status enum, NOT separate tables or duplicated scalar columns. Public reads `published`; owners write `draft`; admin approval copies draft->published and timestamps it. A separate `change_requests` table is the moderation queue / audit log, and `media` handles photo moderation independently.

decisions.question: Where do change-requests and photo moderation live?
decisions.answer: `change_requests` (diff jsonb + decision enum) records what an owner submitted and the admin verdict; `media` (status pending|approved) gates every uploaded photo. The public read layer only ever returns published rows and approved media, so nothing un-moderated can reach the SSG bundle or JSON-LD.

decisions.question: Direct DB array import in app code, or keep generateStaticParams static?
decisions.answer: Keep generateStaticParams returning the full active set (unchanged shape: {id}[]), but source it from a cached getPublishedBusinesses() instead of the literal array. cacheComponents is OFF, so the classic model lets generateStaticParams return the array and pages stay fully prerendered — no Cache Components placeholder-param workaround needed.

decisions.question: Time-based ISR vs on-publish revalidation?
decisions.answer: Both: a long `revalidate` (3600s) as a safety net, plus on-demand revalidatePath + revalidateTag('businesses') fired from the admin approve action / secured webhook so approved edits go live in seconds without a redeploy.

decisions.question: Is the one-time import a migration or an ongoing sync?
decisions.answer: One-time and idempotent. The script imports the businesses array from the .ts file and upserts by id into published+draft, then app code stops importing the array. businesses.ts stays in git as seed/backup only; the DB becomes the source of truth.

decisions.question: How is GDPR/EU residency handled?
decisions.answer: Provision the Supabase project in an EU region so business-owner PII (login email, edited contact details) and uploaded photos are stored in the EU. Service-role key stays server-only; RLS scopes owners to their own data.

audit.notes: Audit facts that shaped this plan: (1) 98 records in /Users/jezza/kamp/src/data/businesses.ts (~11,059 lines), 96 active + 2 status:"closed"; the array is imported synchronously by 26 files (pages, libs, components). (2) Business type lives at the top of the same file (lines 87-183); hours is DayHours[] with HoursPeriod open/close (structured), driving both the "open nu" engine in /Users/jezza/kamp/src/lib/hours.ts and the OpeningHoursSpecification JSON-LD via toOpeningHoursSpec(). perfectFor populated on 96 records, keyFacts on 68, hours on 61, imageUrl on 78; slug/gallery/dietaryTags are declared but unpopulated (id is the de-facto slug; route is /ondernemers/[id]). (3) Read path: /Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/ondernemers/[id]/page.tsx has generateStaticParams over active businesses and emits graph(localBusinessSchema, breadcrumbSchema, faqSchema); JSON-LD builders are in /Users/jezza/kamp/src/lib/schema.ts and must stay untouched. Sitemap (/Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/sitemap.ts) and llms.txt (/Users/jezza/kamp/src/app/llms.txt/route.ts) also iterate the array. (4) next.config.ts is EMPTY — cacheComponents/PPR is NOT enabled, so the classic ISR model (export const revalidate, revalidatePath, revalidateTag, unstable_cache, generateStaticParams may return any array incl. empty) applies directly, with none of the Cache Components "must return >=1 param" constraints. Confirmed against the bundled Next 16 docs in node_modules/next/dist/docs/. (5) Image handling today: /Users/jezza/kamp/src/components/BusinessImage.tsx prefers a local "/"-prefixed owner photo via next/image, else an external http(s) photo via plain , else a generative SVG placeholder — so owner uploads landing in Supabase Storage will need a remotePatterns entry and the approved-only gate. (6) Memory note: heavy multi-agent enrichment runs have twice hit the monthly spend limit — favor the deterministic, single-pass import script over agentic data work. The recommended minimal-diff swap (introduce src/lib/db.ts with cached getPublishedBusiness(es), repoint the 26 importers, leave schema.ts/hours.ts alone) is what guarantees no structured-data or generateStaticParams regression.

audit.area: Admin & Moderation experience for Ondernemers van de Kamp (solo/committee-operated, GDPR/NL, low-cost) — owner login issuance, moderated owner edits & photo uploads, audit log, business-list management, notifications.
audit.recommendation:
Add a small authenticated back-office to the existing Next.js 16 app rather than a separate tool. Keep the public site exactly as it is — pure SSG/ISR reading from a data source — and make every owner edit land in a "pending" queue that the admin approves before it touches what the public pages render. The single highest-leverage architectural decision: move from the hand-edited `src/data/businesses.ts` file to a lightweight hosted Postgres (Neon or Supabase free tier, both EU-region) as the source of truth, but keep the public pages SSG by using a cached read (`use cache` + `cacheTag('businesses')`) and on-demand revalidation (`revalidateTag('businesses')` / `revalidatePath('/ondernemers/[id]')`) fired by the admin's Approve action. This preserves Core Web Vitals and JSON-LD (pages stay prerendered, served from cache) while letting an approval go live in seconds without a Vercel rebuild.

Concrete stack: Auth.js (NextAuth v5) with the Email/"magic link" provider only — no passwords. Postgres on Neon (EU). Photo storage on Vercel Blob (or Supabase Storage). All mutations via Server Actions guarded by a session check. Admin UI is 3–4 server-rendered pages under `/beheer` (NL for "manage"). This is the lightest design that satisfies every requirement and stays inside free/near-free tiers.

KEY FLOWS:

1) Issuing owner logins (email→invite). Admin opens `/beheer/ondernemers`, finds the business (all ~96 already seeded), clicks "Eigenaar uitnodigen", types the owner's email. This writes an `owner_invite` row binding that email to exactly one `businessId` and emails a magic link via Auth.js. Because the invite pre-binds email→business, the owner can only ever edit their own listing — authorization is data-driven, not role-driven. Re-clicking resends. No self-signup: a login only exists if the admin created an invite (or the public /aanmelden form, which stays a lead-gen mailto and is the front door to "request an invite").

2) Owner edits (the moderation core). Logged-in owner sees `/beheer/mijn-zaak` — a form pre-filled with their current published listing covering the editable subset: shortDescription, longDescription, hours (reuse the existing DayHours editor shape), phone, websiteUrl, socials, priceRange/priceLevel, tags, specialties, perfectFor, and photo upload/replace/remove. Saving does NOT mutate the live record. It writes a `pending_edit` row holding a JSON diff (changed fields only) + status `pending`. The public page keeps showing the approved version. Owner sees a banner "Je wijzigingen wachten op goedkeuring."

3) Photo uploads. Uploads go straight to Blob/Storage into a quarantined prefix (e.g. `pending/{businessId}/...`); the URL is attached to the pending_edit, never to the live `imageUrl`. Validate server-side: max ~6MB, JPEG/PNG/WebP only, strip EXIF, re-encode to WebP, cap dimensions (~2000px). On approval the file is promoted to a `live/` prefix and the record's `imageUrl` is set to the local/optimised path so next/image keeps optimising it (the existing BusinessImage precedence already handles `/`-prefixed local photos first). This keeps the permission model the memory file insists on — owner photos only with explicit consent — because the owner uploading IS the consent, and admin still gates publication.

4) Admin moderation dashboard. `/beheer` landing = a queue of pending_edits and pending photos with a count badge. Each item shows a side-by-side diff (current value vs. proposed) and a thumbnail for photos. Admin actions: Approve (applies the diff to the business record, promotes photos, stamps updatedAt, fires revalidation, marks the edit approved), Approve-with-edits (admin tweaks before publishing), or Reject (with an optional reason emailed to the owner). Approval is the ONLY path data reaches the public site — there is no auto-publish.

5) Audit log. Every mutation (invite sent, edit submitted, approved, rejected, business added/closed/featured, photo promoted/removed) appends an immutable `audit_log` row: timestamp, actor (admin email or owner email), businessId, action, and the before/after JSON. Surface it at `/beheer/logboek` as a reverse-chronological feed, filterable by business. This doubles as the GDPR processing record and lets the committee see "who changed what." Never hard-delete; closing a business sets status, and rejected edits stay in the log.

6) Business-list management. `/beheer/ondernemers` is the full ~96-row table with inline controls: Add (create a new business record → starts in a draft/unpublished state until completed), Close (set `status:'closed'` — already excluded from generateStaticParams and detail pages 404, so closures propagate correctly), and Feature (toggle `featured` + adjust `sortOrder`). Each of these is an admin action that also writes audit_log and revalidates.

7) Notifications — keep them email-only and minimal. (a) Owner→admin: on edit submission, email the admin "Nieuwe wijziging van {business} wacht op review" with a deep link to the queue item. (b) Admin→owner: on approve/reject, email the owner the outcome (+ reason on reject). (c) The magic-link login email. Use one transactional provider (Resend free tier, 100/day — ample for 96 owners) wired through Auth.js's email sender too, so there's a single email integration. No SMS, no push, no Slack — overkill for this scale.

WHY NOT just keep editing businesses.ts via PRs/commits from the admin UI: tempting (zero new infra), but it requires a Vercel rebuild per approval (slow, noisy git history), can't safely handle concurrent owner submissions, and gives no clean place for invites/audit/photo-quarantine. The DB approach is barely more infra (one connection string) and is dramatically more robust. If the client truly wants zero DB, the fallback is a single JSON blob in Vercel Blob/KV as the store with the same Server-Action + revalidate flow — but Postgres is recommended.

options.name: Auth.js (NextAuth v5) magic-link + Neon Postgres + Vercel Blob, admin UI in-app under /beheer, Server Actions + on-demand revalidation
options.pros: Lightest stack that meets every requirement; passwordless (no password storage = less GDPR/security surface); same repo/deploy as the site; pending-queue moderation is natural; on-demand revalidateTag keeps pages SSG and CWV intact; all components have generous EU free tiers; one email provider serves both login and notifications.
options.cons: Introduces a DB and migrates source-of-truth away from businesses.ts (one-time seed script needed); Auth.js v5 + Next 16 needs care; you maintain the back-office UI yourself.

options.name: Keep businesses.ts as source of truth; admin UI commits edits via GitHub API → Vercel rebuild
options.pros: No database, no new persistent infra; data stays in git with full history; zero hosting cost beyond Vercel.
options.cons: Rebuild per approval (slow, ~minutes, noisy commits); concurrent owner edits race on one file; awkward place for invites/audit/photo quarantine; secrets (GitHub token) in the app; doesn't scale to live owner self-service.

options.name: Headless CMS (Sanity / Payload / Strapi) as backend + editor
options.pros: Off-the-shelf roles, draft/publish workflow, media handling, and audit history; less custom UI to build; webhooks can trigger revalidation.
options.cons: Heavier to operate and learn for a solo/committee; per-owner row-level 'edit only your own' is awkward in most CMSes; another vendor/account; cost creeps past free tier; data model (~40 fields, DayHours) must be re-modelled in the CMS.

options.name: Google Forms + Sheets + manual admin paste
options.pros: Near-zero build effort; client already understands spreadsheets; trivially cheap.
options.cons: No real auth (anyone with the link), no per-owner scoping, manual photo handling, no integrated audit, error-prone copy-paste back into businesses.ts; fails the 'owner logs in and edits only their listing' goal.

options.name: Supabase (Postgres + Auth + Storage + RLS) all-in-one
options.pros: Single EU vendor for DB, auth, storage; Row-Level Security can enforce owner-only edits at the DB layer; generous free tier; built-in magic links.
options.cons: Slightly more lock-in; RLS + Server Actions adds a learning curve; auth UX is easier to keep cohesive with Auth.js if you also want admin SSO; still need to build the moderation queue UI.

packages: next@16 (existing — Server Actions + use cache/cacheTag + revalidateTag/revalidatePath enable moderated edits without losing SSG)
next-auth@5 (Auth.js — Email/magic-link provider only; passwordless owner + admin login)
@auth/drizzle-adapter or @auth/pg-adapter (session/user persistence)
drizzle-orm + drizzle-kit (typed schema/migrations over Postgres; mirror the existing Business type)
@neondatabase/serverless or postgres (Neon EU Postgres driver) — DB as source of truth
@vercel/blob (quarantined pending/ and live/ photo storage with EXIF strip + WebP re-encode) — or @supabase/storage-js if going Supabase
sharp (server-side image validation, resize, WebP re-encode, EXIF strip)
resend (single transactional email provider for magic links + moderation notifications) + @auth/* email integration
zod (validate owner-submitted form fields and the pending-edit diff payload server-side)
steps: Decide source-of-truth: adopt Neon (EU) Postgres. Write a one-off seed script that imports the current src/data/businesses.ts array into a `businesses` table (keep the TS Business type as the canonical shape / Drizzle schema). Add tables: owner_invites, pending_edits (businessId, ownerEmail, diff jsonb, photoUrls, status, createdAt), audit_log (ts, actor, businessId, action, before jsonb, after jsonb), plus Auth.js user/session tables.

Wrap public data reads in a cached loader: `getBusinesses()` / `getBusiness(id)` with `'use cache'` + `cacheTag('businesses')` (and per-id tag). Repoint all 11 import sites from the static array to this loader. Keep generateStaticParams + JSON-LD unchanged so SSG/SEO is preserved.

Add Auth.js v5 with the Email (magic-link) provider and the Postgres adapter; configure Resend as the email sender. Add an `isAdmin(email)` allowlist (env var of committee emails) and a session helper. Protect /beheer/** with middleware + an in-action session check (Server Actions are publicly POST-able, so verify auth inside every action).
